title: CDC Clinical Practice Guideline for Prescribing Opioids for Pain
content: In addition to updating recommendations on the basis of new evidence regarding management of chronic pain, this clinical practice guideline is intended to assist clinicians in weighing benefits and risks of prescribing opioid pain medication for painful acute conditions (e.g., low back pain, neck pain, other musculoskeletal pain, neuropathic pain, dental pain, kidney stone pain, and acute episodic migraine) and pain related to procedures (e.g., postoperative pain and pain from oral surgery). In 2020, several of these indications were prioritized by an ad hoc committee of the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine (86) as those for which evidence-based clinical practice guidelines would help inform prescribing practices, with the greatest potential effect on public [...] The recommendations aim to improve communication between clinicians and patients about the benefits and risks of prescription opioids and other pain treatment strategies; improve the safety and effectiveness of pain treatment; improve pain, function, and quality of life for persons with pain; and reduce the risks associated with opioid pain treatment (including opioid use disorder, overdose, and death) and with other pain treatment. [...] regulations, and policies also appeared to reflect its recommendations. For example, since 2016, consistent with SUPPORT Act requirements (61), some state Medicaid programs have used the guideline and other resources to promote nonopioid options for chronic pain management (62). Approximately half of all states have passed legislation limiting initial opioid prescriptions for acute pain to a ≤7-day supply (63), and many insurers, pharmacy benefit managers, and pharmacies have enacted similar policies (64). At least 17 states have passed laws requiring or recommending the coprescription of naloxone in the presence of overdose risk factors, such as high dosages of opioids or concomitant opioid pain medications and benzodiazepines (65).

summary_2_sentences: The CDC Clinical Practice Guideline is intended to help clinicians weigh benefits and risks when prescribing opioid pain medication for painful acute conditions and pain related to procedures, including communicating risks and safer use to reduce opioid use disorder, overdose, and death. The guideline’s recommendations build on new evidence on chronic pain management and describes how SUPPORT Act- and state policy-related initiatives have been used to promote nonopioid options and naloxone coprescribing in higher-risk situations.
why_it_matters: For compliance and medical-legal review, the guideline informs institutional opioid prescribing practices and patient risk-communication requirements for acute and procedure-related pain.

title: Development and Update of Guidelines - NCCN
content: The NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology (NCCN Guidelines®) are composed of recommendations for the prevention, diagnosis, and management of malignancies across the continuum of care. The library of NCCN Guidelines® currently apply to more than 97% of people living with cancer or anyone at risk for a diagnosis of cancer in the United States. The NCCN Guidelines incorporate real-time updates in keeping with the rapid advancements in the field of cancer research and management. NCCN Guidelines have also been developed for cancer screening and prevention, supportive care issues important to the comprehensive care of patients with cancer, and recommendations regarding cancer care for specific populations (eg, people living with HIV). The NCCN Guidelines are intended to assist all [...] with HIV). The NCCN Guidelines are intended to assist all individuals who impact decision-making in cancer care, including physicians, nurses, pharmacists, payers, patients and their families, and others. The development of the NCCN Guidelines is an ongoing and iterative process, which is based on a critical review of the best available evidence and derivation of consensus-driven recommendations by multidisciplinary panels of experts in the field of cancer with the valuable perspective of patients included to the greatest extent possible. [...] by the Panel. Because most cancer therapies are associated with adverse effects, the overall balance of therapeutic benefit and safety are weighed by the Panels before making their recommendations. The Panel discussion may or may not result in changes to the Guidelines.

summary_2_sentences: The page describes the NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology as a continuum-of-care set of recommendations for prevention, diagnosis, and management of malignancies, including supportive care and specific populations. It states that guideline development is an ongoing, evidence-based process using multidisciplinary consensus panels, and that NCCN Guidelines incorporate real-time updates reflecting advances in cancer research and management.
why_it_matters: Provides insight into NCCN guideline governance and update methodology that can inform oncology clinical evidence submissions, medical affairs alignment, and safety/benefit consideration in practice.

title: Drugs and Biologics Compendium
content:
View the NCCN Compendium User Guide.

## Creating the NCCN Compendium

NCCN oncology pharmacists generate the NCCN Compendium at the agent level rather than regimen; however, these agents reflect the components of regimens used in disease management. Regimen-level information is available and searchable in a separate column. In addition to Guidelines specific indication and use, NCCN adds relevant information such as pharmacologic class, relevant ICD-10 codes, and FDA indications to the searchable database. Compendium development and maintenance is accomplished by disease type and reviewed by members of the relevant NCCN panel before publication.

### NCCN Compendium Staff [...] menus. As always, the NCCN Compendium continues to be a continuously updated and expanding database in parallel with the NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology (NCCN Guidelines®). [...] NCCN

When autocomplete results are available use up and down arrows to review and enter to select.

# NCCN Drugs & Biologics Compendium

Search The NCCN Drugs & Biologics Compendium®

NCCN Drugs & Biologics Compendium (NCCN Compendium®) contains authoritative, scientifically derived information designed to support decision-making about the appropriate use of drugs and biologics in patients with cancer.

The recommendations for appropriate use of drugs and biologics contained in the NCCN Compendium are extracted directly from the relevant NCCN Guidelines along with their clinical context, route of administration, recommended use, and NCCN category of evidence.

It is organized at the agent level (with regimen-level information available in a separate searchable column) and includes additional fields such as pharmacologic class, relevant ICD-10 codes, and FDA indications.
why_it_matters: For oncology compliance and evidence management, it provides a structured, continuously updated reference aligning drug/biologic use information to NCCN guideline recommendations and associated FDA indication data.

ICH Q5E: Regulatory Guidance for Changes in Manufacturing of Biologics • Over the life of a biopharmaceutical changes are invariable introduced into manufacturing – Improve yield – Changes in sourcing of components – Changes in production scale • Manufacturing changes are governed by ICH Q5E regulation recognized by both the FDA and EMA. – Guidance aims to minimize the drift inherent in a reference product – The regulations provide guidance to conduct a comprehensive assessment on the impact to the product • Key requirements include: – Analytics should be selected and optimized to maximize the likelihood of detecting potential differences – Apply more than one analytical procedure to evaluate the same quality to maximize the detection of potential differences – Evaluate critical control [...] Biosimilar versus Reference Product Regulatory Definitions of a Biosimilar • US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) – A biological product that is highly similar to a US‐licensed reference biological product notwithstanding minor differences in inactive components, and for which there are no clinically meaningful differences in safety, purity, or potency of the product.

title: [PDF] NCCN Guidelines for Patients: Advanced-Stage Prostate Cancer
content:
Everyone with cancer should carefully consider all of the treatment options available for their cancer type, including standard treatments and clinical trials. Talk to your doctor about whether a clinical trial may make sense for you.
Phases Most cancer clinical trials focus on treatment and are done in phases. h Phase 1 trials study the safety and side effects of an investigational drug or treatment approach. h Phase 2 trials study how well the drug or approach works against a specific type of cancer. h Phase 3 trials test the drug or approach against a standard treatment. If the results are good, it may be approved by the FDA. h Phase 4 trials study the safety and benefit of an FDA-approved treatment.
Who can enroll? [...] Knowing all of your options means not missing out on clinical trial opportunities for promising new treatments.
35 NCCN Guidelines for Patients® Advanced-Stage Prostate Cancer, 2026 4 Types of treatment Clinical trials Another way to receive treatment for metastatic prostate cancer may be through a clinical trial.
A clinical trial is a type of medical research study. After being developed and tested in a lab, potential new ways of fighting cancer need to be studied in people.
If found to be safe and effective in a clinical trial, a drug, device, or treatment approach may be approved by the U.S. FDA.

why_it_matters: Provides patient-facing context on clinical trial phases and potential FDA approval, which may inform medical affairs or patient compliance communications but is not a regulatory or submission update.

The AAN has consistently advocated for broad access to healthcare and treatments for patients with neurological conditions and supports administration-led initiatives that increase access. Drug repurposing identifies potential treatments for diseases and conditions by taking into account the established safety profiles of drugs and considering new uses for the drugs. Now, FDA is seeking input on priority areas and potential candidates for drug repurposing. Below are the AAN’s responses to selected questions from the Request for Information. Priority Areas Including chronic neurological conditions is [...] of de novo drug development in this space. Leveraging the established safety profiles, blood-brain barrier penetrance, and pharmacokinetic data of existing medications can significantly accelerate the discovery of agents capable of altering neurotoxic pathways and slowing clinical decline. Conditions under this umbrella must encompass Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), alongside pediatric-onset neurodegenerative disorders like Batten disease. Prioritizing rare and ultra-rare neurological conditions is essential to rectify the structural market failures that leave these small patient populations systematically underserved by traditional pharmaceutical commercialization. By utilizing real-world observational data and established drug [...] and regulatory burden associated with development of pediatric pharmaceuticals. Approaches to Identifying Candidates for Drug Repurposing The AAN recognizes the complex and numerous factors that FDA must consider when selecting a candidate for drug repurposing. To assist in identifying candidates, the AAN recommends that FDA leverage large-scale, federated electronic health record (EHR) databases to systematically mine for off-label utilization signals. In neurology, particularly within rare disorders, clinicians frequently utilize off label therapies to manage complex, refractory symptoms. Applying advanced analytics to large retrospective cohorts (e.g., established datasets of 60,000+ patients with neurological disease) can efficiently surface robust, real-world safety and efficacy

### COMMENTARY

Why Is This Important?  
 Since the 2018 guideline, multiple new biologic and small molecule agents have been approved for Crohn’s disease, and data have emerged supporting earlier initiation of advanced therapies to improve long-term outcomes. The 2025 update reflects this shift toward early treat-to-target strategies, the de-implementation of ineffective agents, and individualized therapy selection based on disease phenotype, prior exposures, and patient preferences.

Key Study Findings  
 The update provides several practice-changing recommendations. [...] Design: Evidence-based clinical practice guideline using the GRADE framework, incorporating systematic literature review and consensus expert opinion.

Setting: Multicenter, multidisciplinary guideline panel convened by the American College of Gastroenterology.

Patients: Adult patients with suspected, newly diagnosed, or established Crohn’s disease, including luminal, fistulizing, and stricturing phenotypes.

Exposure or Interventions: Recommendations encompass diagnostic strategies, dietary and lifestyle interventions, corticosteroids, immunomodulators, biologics, and small molecule therapies, with special attention to new agents approved since the 2018 guidelines. [...] ##### Public Policy

title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) | ACG
content:
Ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease can lead to diarrhea, bleeding, anemia, weight loss, fevers, malnutrition and fistulae. Crohn’s disease can cause scarring of intestines leading to blockage. They also increase risk of intestinal and other types of cancers. IBD can also have extra-intestinal manifestations where areas other than your gastrointestinal system such as your skeletal system, your skin or your eyes may be involved.
 Is diet management important for patients with IBD? [...] + Bowel obstruction: Long-term inflammation can cause scar tissue to form. This can thicken and narrow areas of the bowel, blocking the flow of digestive contents. Medications can reduce inflammation and open the narrowed areas. Others require surgery to remove the diseased portion of the bowel instead.
  + Malnutrition: Symptoms of Crohn’s disease may make eating difficult. Intestinal inflammation can prevent proper absorption of vital nutrients that are needed to stay healthy. [...] What is Crohn’s disease?

  Crohn’s disease is when there is irritation in the intestines. This is a type of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). It can affect anywhere from the mouth to the anus (end of the bowel).

  Crohn’s disease usually occurs in the last part of the small intestine and the beginning of the colon. Sometimes it can show up in patches anywhere in the intestines though. If the irritation goes on for too long it can cause damage. This damage can be scar which narrows the bowel. Also, the body can form a tunnel going from inside the bowel out to the surface. This is called a fistula and can attach the bowels to other body parts like the skin or bladder. When this happens other problems can occur.
 What is the outlook for people with Crohn’s disease?
summary_2_sentences: The ACG topic page describes ulcerative colitis and Crohn’s disease as inflammatory bowel diseases that can cause symptoms such as diarrhea, bleeding, anemia, weight loss, fevers, and malnutrition, and may lead to complications including fistulae and bowel obstruction. It also notes that chronic inflammation can increase risk of intestinal and other cancers and can cause extra-intestinal manifestations.
why_it_matters: General background on IBD complications and progression; not a regulatory or pharmacovigilance update for submission or compliance decisions.

## Acid Reflux / GERD Overview

 What is GERD? [...] + Avoid certain trigger foods and beverages: chocolate, coffee, peppermint, greasy or spicy foods, tomato products, and alcoholic beverages. Some foods and medications trigger GERD (fats, chocolate and caffeinated drinks) and some may irritate the damaged lining of the esophagus (citrus juice, tomato juice, and probably pepper).
  + Stop smoking. Tobacco may result in acid reflux and is one of the biggest risk factors for esophageal cancer.
  + Stop drinking alcohol. It can contribute to acid reflux.
  + Weight loss if overweight. Excess abdominal fat is one of the biggest risk factors for heartburn. Patients who are overweight are significantly more likely to have GERD compared to ideal body weight.
  + Change Eating and Sleeping Habits.
summary_2_sentences: GI.org provides an overview of GERD/acid reflux and lists behavioral recommendations such as avoiding certain trigger foods/drinks (e.g., chocolate, coffee, peppermint, greasy/spicy foods, tomato products, alcohol), stopping smoking, and limiting alcohol. It also notes weight loss for overweight patients and changing eating/sleeping habits to reduce heartburn risk.
why_it_matters: Not a regulatory or compliance item; content is general patient-facing education rather than guidance, safety communication, or approved product policy.

summary_2_sentences: ClinicalTrials.gov lists NCT04880707 (status: recruiting) designated with Fast Track for a study involving third molar (wisdom teeth) extractions and pain management, including evaluation of a “Twin Block” approach and pain medications. The listing notes an impetus to reduce dose and duration of opioid prescriptions for wisdom-tooth pain because even short-term opioid exposures can increase risk of addiction and misuse.
why_it_matters: A Fast Track-designated clinical trial in the opioid-sparing context may inform evidence generation for analgesic strategies used in regulatory submissions and labeling discussions.

title: [Fast Track] Ketone Conferred Resiliency Against Sleep Restriction With Nutritional Intervention.
content:

Ketogenic supplementation is speculated to alleviate some sleep deprivation issues via action of ketones. Ketones are small molecules that appear in the blood when following a ketogenic diet or consuming ketone supplements. The goal of this project is to find out if diet and/or ketones can improve sleep
summary_2_sentences: ClinicalTrials.gov lists NCT06075914 as RECRUITING with a “Fast Track” designation query. The study aims to assess whether diet and/or ketone supplementation can improve sleep in the context of sleep restriction, particularly in military populations.
why_it_matters: A designated recruiting trial may inform evidence planning and regulatory-facing safety/efficacy data generation for ketone or nutritional interventions affecting sleep under sleep deprivation conditions.
